title: San Francisco Demands Apple and Google Delete AI 'Nudify' Apps From App Stores
gist: San Francisco City Attorney David Chiu sent cease-and-desist letters demanding Apple and Google remove 13 AI nudify/face-swap apps and stop profiting from nonconsensual deepfake porn, citing research estimating nudify apps on the two platforms have been downloaded roughly 480 million times and generated about $120 million in revenue, with independent researchers finding most such apps are 'dual-use' tools that evade content moderation.

themes.theme: App-Store Liability for AI Nudify Apps

insights.claim: San Francisco City Attorney David Chiu sent cease-and-desist letters to Apple and Google demanding removal of 13 AI nudify/face-swap apps (8 on the App Store, 5 on the Play Store) and accusing the platforms of aiding and abetting the sale of nonconsensual deepfake pornography.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Marks an escalation from targeting deepfake websites to targeting Apple and Google directly as platform intermediaries, a legal theory that, if successful, creates precedent for app-store liability over third-party AI content generation.
insights.quote: aiding and abetting" the sale of explicit deepfake images and "sever" business relationships with the app developers.
insights.locator: On Thursday, San Francisco city attorney David Chiu sent legal notices

insights.claim: Chiu's office estimates Apple and Google have 'likely made millions of dollars in fees' from nudify apps via in-app payment cuts, positioning the platforms' revenue-share model itself as a legal target under California laws prohibiting support of deepfake pornography services.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Highlights a direct monetization link between app-store commission structures and nonconsensual-content generation, a liability vector for Apple and Google given growing state-level enforcement activism.
insights.quote: made millions of dollars in fees
insights.locator: The city attorney, whose office previously took legal action

insights.claim: Chiu's office previously won legal action against 16 deepfake websites before turning to app stores, indicating a deliberate escalating legal strategy that moves up the distribution chain from content sites to the platforms that host and monetize the apps.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: A repeatable enforcement playbook other state attorneys general could copy, raising the likelihood of similar actions against Apple and Google in other jurisdictions.
insights.locator: The city attorney, whose office previously took legal action against 16 popular deepfake websites

insights.claim: Chiu says his office is prepared to pursue 'all of our legal options' if Apple and Google do not voluntarily strengthen screening systems, signaling potential litigation risk beyond the cease-and-desist stage.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Sets up a probable escalation path to litigation if compliance is inadequate, an event worth monitoring for Apple and Google legal-risk calendars.
insights.quote: if they don't, we will have to consider all of our legal options.

themes.theme: Scale of the Nudify App Economy

insights.claim: Independent research from the Tech Transparency Project in January and April 2026 found roughly 100 nudify-capable apps across the App Store and Play Store that were collectively downloaded around 480 million times and generated an estimated $120 million in combined revenue.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Gives a rough market-size benchmark for the nudify-app economy operating through official app stores, a specific revenue pool at risk from enforcement and removal actions.
insights.quote: Apps identified by the research were estimated to have been collectively downloaded around 480 million times and may have made around $120 million in combined revenues.
insights.locator: In January and April this year, the Tech Transparency Project

insights.claim: xAI's Grok chatbot was used to generate millions of sexualized nonconsensual images in January 2026, cited as the most transparent, high-profile example of AI nudification technology reaching mass scale.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Flags xAI and Grok as carrying direct reputational and potential legal exposure on this issue, relevant for anyone tracking xAI or X Corp risk alongside Apple and Google's app-store liability.
insights.quote: most transparently with xAI's Grok being used to create millions of sexualized images in January

insights.claim: One of the 13 apps targeted by San Francisco has more than 1 million downloads and openly advertises sexualized image styles on its marketing site despite app-store policies nominally banning pornography.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Illustrates the gap between stated app-store content policies and actual enforcement at the point of app discovery and marketing, a discoverable due-diligence signal for assessing platform trust-and-safety claims.
insights.quote: bikini queen curvy," "calm busty," and "cinematic intimacy.
insights.locator: The 13 apps investigated by the City Attorney's Office

insights.claim: A Cornell/Georgetown preprint (May 2026) found 420 general face-swap apps on Apple's and Google's app stores; of 155 tested, 70 percent could generate nude face-swaps despite none being advertised as nudification tools, evidence these are 'dual-use' apps designed to evade content moderation by presenting as benign.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Establishes a structural moderation blind spot in dual-use app design that undermines app-store safety claims and could become the basis for future liability standards for AI-content apps generally, not just nudify tools.
insights.quote: face swap apps... are effectively 'dual-use': apps that evade content moderation by platforms because they present as benign, but possess the capability to create harmful content.
insights.locator: researchers from Cornell University and Georgetown University identified 420 apps

insights.claim: Google says it has deleted 'hundreds' of nudifying apps for policy violations and restricted search terms like 'nudify,' yet the Tech Transparency Project's director says the problem recurred at similar or worse scale after a second report, indicating current platform self-policing is reactive and insufficient.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Suggests platform self-policing has structurally failed to keep pace with new app submissions, supporting the case for external regulatory pressure rather than reliance on platform self-enforcement.
insights.quote: We didn't think after the first report that we would see this as a problem again—and it was just as bad, if not worse, after the second report.
insights.locator: says Katie Paul, the director of TTP

insights.claim: Prior WIRED and Indicator Media reporting has found deepfake sexual abuse images created of minors in at least 90 US schools, indicating the nudify-app harm extends into K-12 environments, not just adult targets.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Broadens the liability and policy-risk surface for app-store operators to include child-safety law exposure, which tends to draw the most aggressive state and federal enforcement.
insights.quote: Previous reporting by WIRED and Indicator Media has uncovered incidents in at least 90 schools where deepfake sexual abuse images have been created of minors.
insights.locator: Often all it takes to create sexual deepfakes
